mcq.q: The stomach of a fowl in which ENZYMATIC (chemical) digestion takes place is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Proventriculus

mcq.q: In a digestibility trial, a cow excreted 35 kg of manure with a moisture content of 55%. The DRY MATERIAL content of the manure is ... kg.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. 15,75

mcq.q: The following is TRUE about digestion in the stomach of a pig: \(i\) Pancreatic juice neutralises acidic chyme \(ii\) Pepsin breaks down proteins into peptones \(iii\) Rennin converts soluble caseinogen into insoluble casein \(iv\) Inactive pepsinogen is changed into active pepsin
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. (i), (ii) and (iv)

mcq.q: Part ... of the villi transports digested fats and fat-soluble vitamins into the LYMPHATIC SYSTEM.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Lacteal (Y)

mcq.q: The rumen micro-organism that digests CELLULOSE by secreting the enzyme cellulase is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Cellulolytic bacteria

mcq.q: ratio and is best suited for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Narrow; milk production and growth

mcq.q: The IDEAL temperature inside the rumen for optimal microbial activity is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. 39°C -- 40°C

mcq.q: An animal ingested 20 kg of hay with a moisture content of 60%. The DRY MATTER intake is ... kg.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. 8

mcq.q: Biological Value (BV) of an IDEAL protein is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. 100

mcq.q: The enzyme SALIVARY AMYLASE is secreted in the MOUTH of a pig. Its function is to change ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Starch into maltose

mcq.q: The CARDIAC region of the non-ruminant stomach mainly functions to ...
opts: Control exit of food into small intestine
Secrete mucus to protect stomach lining against HCl
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Secrete mucus to protect stomach lining against HCl

mcq.q: Vitamin E deficiency in young animals causes ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Stiff lamb disease

mcq.q: Iodine deficiency in farm animals results in ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Goitre

mcq.q: METABOLIC ENERGY (ME) is calculated by subtracting from Gross Energy all of the following EXCEPT ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Energy used in physical activity

mcq.q: Urea is classified as a Non-Protein Nitrogen (NPN) source. The MAIN risk of feeding urea is that ...
opts: It is more expensive than soya meal
Excess urea converts to ammonia, which is poisonous
It dissolves too slowly for rumen bacteria
It cannot be used by any farm animal
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Excess urea converts to ammonia, which is poisonous

mcq.q: In ruminants, the breakdown of cellulose (fibre) is made possible mainly by ...
opts: Micro-organisms (bacteria and protozoa) in the rumen that secrete cellulase
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ruminants themselves cannot produce cellulase; symbiotic rumen micro-organisms break down cellulose into usable nutrients.

mcq.q: The end products of microbial fermentation of carbohydrates in the rumen are mainly ...
opts: Volatile fatty acids (VFAs) such as acetic, propionic and butyric acid, plus methane and carbon dioxide gas
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. VFAs are absorbed through the rumen wall and used as the animal's main energy source.

mcq.q: Ruminant animals can utilise non-protein nitrogen sources such as UREA because ...
opts: Urea is directly absorbed as protein
Rumen micro-organisms can convert urea (a nitrogen source) into microbial protein, which the animal later digests
Urea is toxic and never used
Non-ruminants also use urea the same way
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Microbes use urea-nitrogen to build their own protein, which is digested by the animal further down the alimentary canal.

mcq.q: Bloat in ruminants is a digestive disorder caused mainly by ...
opts: Too little roughage in the diet
Excessive gas (from fermentation) trapped in the rumen, often due to feeding on lush legume pasture, which cannot be expelled by belching (eructation)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Bloat is dangerous as the swollen rumen can press on the lungs/diaphragm, restricting breathing.

mcq.q: Acidosis in ruminants is commonly caused by ...
opts: Too much roughage in the diet
A sudden, excessive intake of highly fermentable concentrates/grain, causing excess lactic acid production and a drop in rumen pH
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Rapid fermentation of starch-rich feed produces excess lactic acid, lowering rumen pH and harming the animal.

mcq.q: The rumen, the largest compartment of a ruminant's stomach, primarily functions as a site of ...
opts: Microbial fermentation and storage of feed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The rumen acts as a fermentation vat; true chemical (enzymatic) digestion occurs later, in the abomasum.

mcq.q: The reticulum functions mainly to ...
opts: Trap foreign objects/heavy materials and aid in the formation of the cud for rumination
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The honeycomb-structured reticulum helps form the bolus (cud) that is regurgitated for further chewing.

mcq.q: The omasum functions mainly to ...
opts: Absorb water and some nutrients from the digesta, and further grind feed between its many leaf-like folds
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The omasum's folded structure increases surface area for water absorption and mechanical breakdown.

mcq.q: The abomasum is referred to as the 'true stomach' of a ruminant because it ...
opts: Is the only compartment where true enzymatic/chemical digestion occurs, similar to a monogastric stomach
Stores feed for the longest period
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The abomasum secretes HCl and digestive enzymes (e.g. pepsin, rennin), comparable to the single stomach of non-ruminants.

mcq.q: Rumination ('chewing the cud') refers to the process where ...
opts: Feed passes straight through the gut without re-chewing
Partially digested feed (cud) is regurgitated from the rumen back to the mouth for further chewing, mixing with saliva, before being re-swallowed
Saliva is produced without feed intake
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Rumination increases the surface area of feed particles, improving microbial fermentation efficiency.

mcq.q: Which nutrient is the primary and most efficient source of ENERGY in livestock feed?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Carbohydrates (e.g. starches, sugars, fibre) are broken down to provide the bulk of an animal's energy requirements.

mcq.q: Protein in the diet is primarily required for ...
opts: Growth, tissue repair and the production of products such as milk, wool, eggs and muscle (meat)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Protein supplies amino acids essential for building and repairing body tissues and producing animal products.

mcq.q: Fats (lipids) in animal feed function mainly to ...
opts: Provide the lowest energy density of all nutrients
Provide a concentrated source of energy (more than double that of carbohydrates per gram) and supply essential fatty acids
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Fats yield roughly 2.25 times more energy per gram than carbohydrates or protein.

mcq.q: Calcium and phosphorus are minerals primarily required by livestock for ...
opts: Bone and teeth development, and (calcium) for milk production and blood clotting
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These two minerals work together and must be supplied in the correct ratio for healthy bone development and lactation.

mcq.q: A deficiency of calcium in dairy cows around calving can lead to a condition known as ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Milk fever results from a sudden drop in blood calcium as large amounts are diverted into colostrum/milk production at calving.

mcq.q: Vitamins are required by animals in ... amounts, but are essential for ...
opts: Small; normal metabolic functions, growth and disease resistance
No amount; they have no real function
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Although needed only in tiny quantities, vitamin deficiencies can cause serious metabolic and health problems.

mcq.q: Water is often considered the MOST essential nutrient because ...
opts: Animals can survive longest without it compared to other nutrients
It is involved in nearly all body processes (digestion, temperature regulation, nutrient transport) and animals can survive only a short time without it
It is only needed by lactating animals
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. While animals can survive weeks without food, they can only survive days without water.

mcq.q: Roughage (e.g. hay, grass, silage) is characterised by ...
opts: High energy density and low fibre content
High fibre (cellulose) content and relatively low energy density compared to concentrates
Being suitable only for monogastric animals
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Roughage is bulky, high in fibre, and essential for maintaining healthy rumen function in ruminants.

mcq.q: Concentrates (e.g. maize, grain-based feeds) are characterised by ...
opts: High energy and/or protein content and relatively low fibre content
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Concentrates are energy- and/or protein-dense feeds used to supplement roughage, especially for high-producing animals.

mcq.q: A balanced ration is best described as a diet that ...
opts: Contains only one type of nutrient in excess
Supplies all required nutrients (energy, protein, minerals, vitamins, water) in the correct proportions and amounts needed for an animal's specific requirements
Is the same for all classes of livestock regardless of age or production stage
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Balanced rations are formulated according to an animal's species, age, production stage (e.g. growth, lactation) and purpose.

mcq.q: Feed Conversion Ratio (FCR) is calculated as ...
opts: Total weight gain ÷ total feed consumed
Total feed consumed (kg) ÷ total weight gain (kg)
Total feed cost ÷ number of animals
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A LOWER FCR indicates more efficient feed conversion (less feed needed per kg of weight gain).

practice.q: Differentiate between a RUMINANT and NON-RUMINANT based on structural differences in the stomach.
practice.memo:
• Ruminant: has a complex stomach with FOUR compartments (Rumen, Reticulum, Omasum, Abomasum)
• Non-ruminant: has a SIMPLE stomach with only ONE compartment

practice.q: Explain TWO differences in the FEEDS utilised by ruminants and non-ruminants.
practice.memo:
• Ruminants CAN feed on roughage because rumen micro-organisms digest cellulose; Non-ruminants CANNOT (no rumen/micro-organisms)
• Ruminants CAN utilise urea (converted to microbial protein); Non-ruminants CANNOT --- excess urea converts to toxic ammonia

practice.q: Name the THREE pairs of salivary glands in the mouth of a pig and state where each is situated.
practice.memo:
• Parotid gland --- situated below/in front of the ear
• Sublingual gland --- situated below the tongue
• Submandibular (sub-maxillary) gland --- situated between the parotid and sublingual glands

practice.q: State the function of each of the following stomach regions of a NON-RUMINANT: **(i)** Cardiac region
practice.memo:
• Cardiac region: secretes mucus to protect the stomach lining against HCl
• Fundic region: secretes hydrochloric acid (HCl), renin and pepsin
• Pyloric region: controls the exit of food from the stomach into the small intestine

practice.q: Name the ENZYME and state its FUNCTION for each of the following: **(i)** Mouth (salivary amylase)
practice.memo:
• Mouth --- Salivary amylase: changes starch into maltose
• Stomach --- Renin: changes caseinogen into casein
• Stomach --- Pepsin: changes protein into peptones
• Small intestine --- Sucrase: changes sucrose into glucose and fructose
• Small intestine --- Lipase: changes lipids into fatty acids and glycerol

practice.q: Distinguish between ACTIVE ABSORPTION and PASSIVE ABSORPTION. Give ONE example of each.
practice.memo:
• Active absorption: requires ENERGY; moves nutrients from LOW to HIGH concentration (against gradient). Example: active transport of glucose
• Passive absorption: does NOT require energy; moves nutrients from HIGH to LOW concentration (along gradient). Example: diffusion/osmosis

practice.q: State the function of EACH of the following parts of the alimentary canal of a FOWL: **(i)** Beak
practice.memo:
• Beak: collects/picks up food particles (ingestion)
• Crop: moistens, soaks, softens and stores food
• Proventriculus: secretes gastric juice (HCl, renin, pepsin) for chemical digestion
• Gizzard (Ventriculus): grinds food using small stones (mechanical digestion)
• Cloaca: expels both urine and faeces (excretion)

practice.q: Describe the PATHWAY of food in the ruminant's stomach from oesophagus back to the mouth (regurgitation) -- name all processes involved.
practice.memo:
• Food moves from oesophagus by PERISTALSIS into the rumen
• Food REGURGITATES from the reticulo-rumen back to the mouth for re-chewing (rumination)
• After re-chewing, food is swallowed again and moved by RETRO-PERISTALSIS into the omasum and abomasum

practice.q: Name and explain the function of EACH of the four stomach compartments of a RUMINANT.
practice.memo:
• Rumen: largest compartment; contains papillae and micro-organisms (bacteria/protozoa) for microbial digestion of cellulose; heat production
• Reticulum: 'honeycomb'/hardware stomach; traps and stores foreign objects (e.g. nails, wire)
• Omasum: 'leaf' stomach; many folds absorb water and grind/filter food particles
• Abomasum: TRUE stomach/milk stomach; contains gastric glands, HCl, renin & pepsin --- site of chemical digestion

practice.q: Name the THREE types of bacteria found in the rumen and state the function of EACH.
practice.memo:
• Cellulolytic bacteria: digest cellulose using the enzyme cellulase; increase with high-fibre diets
• Amylolytic bacteria: digest starch using the enzyme maltase; increase with high-starch diets
• Proteolytic bacteria: digest protein; increase with high-protein diets

practice.q: State FIVE requirements/ideal conditions for rumen micro-organisms to function optimally.
practice.memo:
• Suitable pH (around 6,4)
• Suitable temperature (39°C -- 40°C)
• Sufficient nitrogen source (e.g. from urea)
• Regular intake of feed
• Oxygen-free (anaerobic) environment

practice.q: State FOUR functions of rumen micro-organisms.
practice.memo:
• Digest cellulose/roughage to form fatty acids, CO₂ and methane
• Synthesise Vitamin B-complex and Vitamin K
• Synthesise protein (microbial protein from urea/NPN)
• Digest starch into maltose

practice.q: Name SIX essential nutrients that animals require in their diet.
practice.memo: • Water, Carbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids (Fats and Oils), Minerals, Vitamins

practice.q: Explain why an animal drinks MORE water and eats LESS on a HOT day.
practice.memo: • More water helps the animal COOL DOWN through evaporation/sweating; less feed reduces internal heat production from digestion

practice.q: Name the BUILDING BLOCK of each of the following nutrients: (i) Carbohydrates (ii) Protein (iii) Fats and Oils.
practice.memo:
• Carbohydrates: Glucose (simple sugars)
• Protein: Amino acids
• Fats and Oils: Fatty acids and glycerol

practice.q: Give the DEFICIENCY DISEASE for each of the following vitamins: (i) Vitamin A (ii) Vitamin D (iii) Vitamin E (iv) Vitamin K (v) Vitamin B1 (vi) Vitamin B2 (vii) Vitamin B12.
practice.memo:
• Vitamin A: Night blindness / Keratomalacia
• Vitamin D: Rickets / Osteomalacia
• Vitamin E: Stiff lamb disease (white muscle disease)
• Vitamin K: Poor blood clotting
• Vitamin B1: Polyneuritis
• Vitamin B2: Curled-toe paralysis
• Vitamin B12: Wasting disease / poor hatchability in poultry

practice.q: Give the DEFICIENCY CONDITION for each of the following minerals: (i) Calcium/Phosphorus (ii) Sodium (iii) Iodine (iv) Iron (v) Zinc (vi) Copper (vii) Cobalt.
practice.memo:
• Calcium/Phosphorus: Rickets and Osteomalacia
• Sodium: Pica (animal eats non-feed objects)
• Iodine: Goitre (enlarged thyroid gland)
• Iron: Anaemia
• Zinc: Parakeratosis (rough skin in pigs)
• Copper: Swayback / hairlessness (keratinization)
• Cobalt: Wasting disease

practice.q: Define DIGESTIBILITY and DIGESTIBILITY COEFFICIENT.
practice.memo:
• Digestibility: the proportion of feed that is NOT excreted in the faeces but absorbed/retained by the body
• Digestibility Coefficient: the proportion of a nutrient consumed and absorbed, expressed as a PERCENTAGE

practice.q: Interpret the following digestibility coefficient results: (i) above 70% (ii) 60--70% (iii) 40--59% (iv) less than 40%.
practice.memo:
• Above 70%: very high/good digestibility
• 60--70%: high/moderate digestibility
• 40--59%: low digestibility
• Less than 40%: very low digestibility

practice.q: Define BIOLOGICAL VALUE (BV) of protein. Name THREE factors that determine it.
practice.memo:
• BV: an index used to measure the QUALITY of protein in a feed
• Determined by: amino acid composition / ratio of amino acids / amount of nitrogen retained by the body (any 3)

practice.q: Distinguish between ESSENTIAL and NON-ESSENTIAL amino acids.
practice.memo:
• Essential amino acid: CANNOT be synthesised by the animal's body --- must be supplied through feed
• Non-essential amino acid: CAN be synthesised by the animal's own body

practice.q: Define EACH of the following energy values: (i) Gross Energy (ii) Digestible Energy (iii) Metabolic Energy (iv) Net Energy.
practice.memo:
• Gross Energy (GE): total potential energy of feed; energy released when feed is completely combusted
• Digestible Energy (DE): GE minus energy lost in faeces (manure)
• Metabolic Energy (ME): GE minus energy lost in faeces, urine and gases (CH₄, CO₂)
• Net Energy (NE): GE minus energy lost in faeces, urine, gases AND body heat

practice.q: Define NUTRITIVE RATIO. Distinguish between a NARROW and WIDE nutritive ratio, including uses of each.
practice.memo:
• Nutritive Ratio: a measure of the PROTEIN content of a feed (ratio of digestible energy-yielding nutrients to digestible protein)
• NARROW (below 1:6): HIGH protein --- used for PRODUCTION (growth, milk, reproduction)
• WIDE (above 1:6): LOW protein --- used for FATTENING and MAINTENANCE

practice.q: List FOUR differences between roughage and concentrate (% TDN, % crude fibre, cost, bulkiness).
practice.memo:
• % TDN: Roughage = LOW; Concentrate = HIGH
• % Crude fibre: Roughage = HIGH; Concentrate = LOW
• Cost: Roughage = CHEAP; Concentrate = EXPENSIVE
• Bulkiness: Roughage = BULKY; Concentrate = LESS BULKY

practice.q: An animal ingested 30 kg of grass with a moisture content of 60%. 10 kg of feed was excreted in the faeces with a moisture content of 75%. Calculate the DIGESTIBILITY COEFFICIENT. Show ALL working.
practice.memo:
• Moisture intake = 60/100 × 30 kg = 18 kg → Dry matter intake = 30 − 18 = 12 kg
• Moisture excreted = 75/100 × 10 kg = 7,5 kg → Dry matter excreted = 10 − 7,5 = 2,5 kg
• DC% = (DM intake − DM excreted)/DM intake × 100 = (12 − 2,5)/12 × 100 = 79,17%
• Interpretation: Above 70% = very high digestibility

practice.q: Using the PEARSON SQUARE METHOD, balance a ration of maize (8% DP) and soya bean meal (44% DP) to meet a requirement of 18% DP. Then calculate the PERCENTAGE of each ingredient in the mixture.
practice.memo:
• Maize parts = 44 − 18 = 26 parts; Soya parts = 18 − 8 = 10 parts; Total = 36 parts
• % Maize = 26/36 × 100 = 72,2% ①1.5
• % Soya meal = 10/36 × 100 = 27,8% ①1.5

practice.q: A feed has 80% TDN and 20% DP. Calculate the NUTRITIVE RATIO and state whether it is NARROW or WIDE.
practice.memo:
• NR = 1 : (TDN − DP)/DP = 1 : (80 − 20)/20 = 1 : 60/20 = 1 : 3
• 1:3 is a NARROW nutritive ratio (below 1:6)

practice.q: Name and briefly describe FOUR types of growth stimulants used in animal production.
practice.memo:
• Tranquiliser/Sedative: keeps animals calm so they eat more feed and gain weight faster
• Antibiotics: prevent disease which could otherwise reduce growth rate
• Thyroid regulator: stimulates the animal's metabolism and growth rate
• Hormones: growth hormone pellets implanted under the skin to stimulate growth

practice.q: Define a FODDER FLOW PROGRAMME and name FOUR factors to consider when planning one.
practice.memo:
• Fodder flow programme: a strategic plan to ensure SUFFICIENT feed is available for animals throughout the year
• Factors: number of animals / type of animal / seasonal feed availability / cost of feed (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Describe how micro-organisms in the rumen enable a ruminant to digest cellulose (fibre) that monogastric animals cannot digest.
practice.memo:
• Rumen micro-organisms (bacteria and protozoa) produce the enzyme cellulase ✓
• Cellulase breaks down cellulose into simpler carbohydrates ✓
• These are further fermented into volatile fatty acids (VFAs) ✓
• VFAs are absorbed through the rumen wall and used by the animal as an energy source ✓

practice.q: Explain the cause of bloat in cattle and describe ONE way a farmer can prevent it.
practice.memo:
• Cause: rapid fermentation of lush, leguminous pasture produces excessive gas that becomes trapped in the rumen (often as a stable foam) and cannot be belched out ✓✓
• Prevention: avoid allowing animals to graze excessively on wet, lush legume pasture; introduce legumes gradually; provide roughage/hay before grazing; use anti-bloat treatments/drenches ✓✓ (any valid prevention method)

practice.q: Explain why a sudden change from a roughage-based diet to a high-concentrate (grain) diet can cause acidosis in cattle.
practice.memo:
• Rumen micro-organisms ferment the readily available starch in grain very rapidly ✓
• This produces excess lactic acid ✓
• Rumen pH drops sharply (becomes too acidic) ✓
• This harms/kills the normal rumen micro-organism population and damages the rumen wall, making the animal ill ✓

practice.q: Name the FOUR compartments of a ruminant's stomach, in the order that feed passes through them, and state ONE function of each.
practice.memo:
• Rumen — fermentation/storage of feed by micro-organisms ✓✓
• Reticulum — traps foreign objects, aids cud formation ✓✓
• Omasum — absorbs water, further grinds feed ✓✓
• Abomasum — true enzymatic digestion (HCl, pepsin) ✓✓

practice.q: Tabulate FOUR nutrient classes required in animal nutrition and state ONE function of each.
practice.memo:
• Carbohydrates — main source of energy ✓
• Protein — growth, tissue repair, production of milk/meat/wool ✓
• Fats — concentrated energy source, supplies essential fatty acids ✓
• Minerals (e.g. Ca, P) — bone/teeth development ✓ (any 4, vitamins/water also accepted)

practice.q: A pig consumed 240 kg of feed over a period during which it gained 60 kg in body weight. Calculate the pig's Feed Conversion Ratio (FCR) and explain what this value indicates about the pig's feed efficiency.
practice.memo:
• FCR = Feed consumed ÷ Weight gain = 240 kg ÷ 60 kg = 4 ✓✓
• This means 4 kg of feed was required to produce 1 kg of weight gain ✓
• A lower FCR would indicate more efficient (better) feed conversion; this value should be compared to breed/industry standards to assess efficiency ✓

practice.q: Explain the cause of milk fever in dairy cows and describe how it can be prevented/treated.
practice.memo:
• Cause: a sudden drop in blood calcium levels around calving, as large amounts of calcium are diverted to colostrum/milk production faster than it can be mobilised from the bones/absorbed from the diet ✓✓
• Prevention/treatment: feed a calcium-balanced (often a low-calcium, high-phosphorus 'pre-calving') ration before calving to stimulate calcium mobilisation mechanisms; administer a calcium injection/infusion if symptoms appear ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between roughage and concentrates as types of livestock feed, giving an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Roughage: high fibre, bulky, lower energy density, e.g. hay/grass/silage ✓✓
• Concentrates: low fibre, high energy and/or protein density, e.g. maize/grain/oilcake meal ✓✓

mcq.q: An INTENSIVE production system is best characterised by ...
opts: Low capital, large land, animals grazing naturally
High capital, small land, controlled environment for maximum output
Low output, no technology, subsistence goal
Animals kept in veld on natural pasture only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. High capital, small land, controlled environment for maximum output

mcq.q: The picture below shows a layer housing system. This system is called a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Battery cage system

mcq.q: spreads RAPIDLY through an area is a/an ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Epidemic

mcq.q: Animal housing should be oriented so that the LONG SIDE of the building faces EAST. The reason for this is to ensure ...
opts: The building is warm in winter and cool in summer
Animals face the sun during feeding
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The building is warm in winter and cool in summer

mcq.q: The BALANCE POINT in the flight zone of an animal is the point that ... crossed
opts: The animal will kick if approached from behind
Is beyond the flight zone where handler is safe
At the shoulder that controls forward or backward movement when
Is the centre of the blind spot
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. At the shoulder that controls forward or backward movement when

mcq.q: Which of the following is an ABNORMAL behaviour shown by pigs under stress?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Tail biting and ear biting

mcq.q: record motion (oestrus detection) is a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Pedometer

mcq.q: Animal housing and shelter plays a vital role in protecting animals against which CORRECT combination?
opts: Extreme heat, severe cold and wetting by rain
Extreme heat, proper breeding and wetting by rain
All four: heat, cold, breeding and rain
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Extreme heat, severe cold and wetting by rain

mcq.q: The CRUSH is a piece of equipment used in animal handling. Its PRIMARY purpose is to ... pregnancy testing
opts: Restrain large animals for medical treatment, dehorning, or
Weigh animals accurately on a scale
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Restrain large animals for medical treatment, dehorning, or

mcq.q: Which of the following loading RAMP requirements is INCORRECT?
opts: The ramp should not be too steep
The ramp should have non-slip grooves
The ramp should be very narrow to prevent animals from turning
The ramp should be adjustable to different vehicle heights
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. The ramp should be very narrow to prevent animals from turning

mcq.q: The sequential stages in the development of the LIVER FLUKE parasite are ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Egg → Miracidium → Cercaria → Adult fluke

mcq.q: Mastitis is the inflammation of the ... that occurs in ...
opts: Skin; sheep infested by mange mites
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Udder; dairy cattle

mcq.q: The BLUE TICK is a one-host tick. The disease it causes in cattle is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Red-water (Babesiosis) and Gall-sickness (Anaplasmosis)

mcq.q: is classified as a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Zoonotic disease

mcq.q: UREA POISONING in farm animals is caused by ...
opts: Animals eating grass treated with herbicide
Animals ingesting toxic plants such as thorn apple
Excess urea intake that converts to ammonia, poisoning the animal
Maize stored in wet conditions developing aflatoxin mould
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Excess urea intake that converts to ammonia, poisoning the animal

mcq.q: The TREATMENT for urea poisoning in farm animals is to administer ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Vinegar

mcq.q: The microscopic external parasite with FOUR pairs of legs and a sucking mouth that causes MANGE and SCAB in farm animals is a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Mite

mcq.q: Ear tagging is a method of animal identification used mainly to ...
opts: Allow individual animals to be identified for record-keeping (e.g. breeding, health, production records)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Permanent identification is essential for accurate farm records and traceability.

mcq.q: Branding (hot iron or freeze branding) as a method of identification has the disadvantage of ...
opts: Causing stress/pain to the animal and potentially damaging the hide/skin value
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. While branding provides a permanent, easily visible mark, it causes some pain and can devalue hides.

mcq.q: A tattoo, as a method of livestock identification, is typically applied to the ...
opts: Inner ear or lip, providing a permanent mark
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Tattoos are long-lasting and relatively low-cost but can be difficult to read from a distance.

mcq.q: Microchipping as an identification method has the main advantage of ...
opts: Being visible to the naked eye from far away
Providing a permanent, tamper-proof electronic identification that can be scanned for instant, accurate record retrieval
Being the cheapest identification method available
Requiring no special equipment to read
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Microchips are read using a scanner, offering high accuracy and reducing the risk of identification fraud/loss.

mcq.q: Accurate farm records (e.g. production, health, breeding records) are important mainly because they ...
opts: Have no effect on farm management decisions
Help the farmer make informed management and breeding decisions, monitor productivity, and identify problems early
Are only required for legal/tax purposes
Replace the need for animal identification
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Good records allow farmers to track performance, plan breeding programmes and detect health/production issues promptly.

mcq.q: Vaccination works by ...
opts: Directly killing the disease-causing pathogen in an infected animal
Stimulating an animal's immune system to produce antibodies, providing future protection/immunity against a specific disease
Replacing the need for good nutrition
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Vaccines expose the immune system to a weakened/inactive form of a pathogen (or its antigens), training it to respond quickly to future exposure.

mcq.q: Quarantine of newly purchased animals is an important biosecurity measure because it ...
opts: Increases the spread of disease to the rest of the herd
Allows new animals to be observed and isolated for a period to ensure they are not carrying a disease before introducing them to the main herd
Is required only for export animals
Has no effect on disease control
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Quarantine periods help prevent the introduction of new diseases into an established, healthy herd/flock.

mcq.q: Dipping or spraying cattle with an acaricide is mainly done to control ...
opts: External parasites such as ticks, which can transmit diseases like redwater and heartwater
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ticks are vectors for several serious livestock diseases, making tick control a key part of herd health management.

mcq.q: Deworming (drenching) of livestock is done to control ...
opts: Internal parasites such as roundworms and tapeworms, which compete for nutrients and damage internal organs
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Regular deworming programmes reduce the negative impact of internal parasites on growth and production.

mcq.q: Castration of male livestock not intended for breeding is commonly performed in order to ...
opts: Reduce aggressive behaviour, improve meat quality/tenderness, and prevent unwanted breeding
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Castrated males (e.g. steers, wethers) are typically calmer and easier to manage, and their meat is often considered higher quality.

mcq.q: Dehorning of cattle is commonly practised mainly to ...
opts: Increase the animal's ability to defend itself
Reduce the risk of injury to other animals and handlers, and reduce space needed in feedlots/transport
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Hornless (polled) or dehorned cattle are generally safer to handle and house together.

mcq.q: Biosecurity on a farm refers to ...
opts: A set of management practices designed to prevent the introduction and spread of disease-causing organisms onto and within a farm
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Biosecurity includes measures such as quarantine, controlled farm access, hygiene protocols and pest control.

mcq.q: Foot-and-mouth disease (FMD) is a highly contagious ... disease affecting cloven-hoofed animals (e.g. cattle, sheep, pigs).
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. FMD is caused by a virus and can spread rapidly, having serious trade and economic implications, requiring strict control measures.

mcq.q: An extensive livestock production system is characterised by ...
opts: High stocking density, animals confined indoors, and high input costs
Low stocking density, animals grazing over large areas of natural/cultivated pasture, with relatively low input costs
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Extensive systems rely largely on natural resources (veld/pasture) rather than purchased feed.

mcq.q: An intensive livestock production system is characterised by ...
opts: Low stocking density and minimal management
High stocking density, animals often confined (e.g. feedlots, battery cages), high input costs but also typically higher output/productivity per animal
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Intensive systems maximise production through controlled environments, formulated feed and close management, at higher cost.

mcq.q: A feedlot system for finishing cattle is an example of ... production.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Feedlots confine cattle at high density and feed concentrated rations to maximise weight gain quickly before slaughter.

mcq.q: The 'Five Freedoms' of animal welfare include freedom from hunger and thirst, freedom from discomfort, freedom from pain/injury/disease, freedom to express normal behaviour, and ...
opts: Freedom to roam unrestricted at all times
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These five freedoms form an internationally recognised framework for assessing and ensuring animal welfare.

mcq.q: Stocking rate refers to ...
opts: The price received per animal sold
The number of animals kept per unit area of land (e.g. per hectare)
The total feed cost per year
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Correct stocking rate is essential to prevent overgrazing while making efficient use of available grazing land.

mcq.q: Overstocking (exceeding the correct carrying capacity of land) commonly leads to ...
opts: Overgrazing, soil erosion, and declining pasture/veld condition and animal productivity
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Overstocking degrades the grazing resource, ultimately reducing the land's long-term productive capacity.

mcq.q: Rotational grazing is a management practice where ...
opts: Animals graze the same camp continuously without rest
Livestock are systematically moved between different camps/paddocks, allowing grazed areas time to rest and recover
Only one camp is ever used
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Rotational grazing helps maintain pasture quality and prevents overgrazing of any single area.

practice.q: Distinguish between INTENSIVE and EXTENSIVE production systems using the following aspects: goal, capital investment, area of land, feeding and environmental effects.
practice.memo:
• Goal: making profit
• Capital investment: HIGH
• Area of land: occupies a SMALL area
• Feeding: animals fed specially formulated feeds in feedlots/units
• Environmental effects: MORE environmental impact  *EXTENSIVE:*
• Goal: minimising risk
• Capital investment: LOW
• Area of land: occupies a LARGE area
• Feeding: animals graze on natural pasture in the veld
• Environmental effects: LESS environmental impact

practice.q: Explain THREE factors that must be controlled under an INTENSIVE production system.
practice.memo:
• Feeding: farmer must produce/buy feed suitable for growth and production
• Environment: shelter must be provided with controlled heating, cooling and ventilation
• Breeding: farmer selects animals with desired characteristics (high quality products, disease resistance)

practice.q: Distinguish between SUBSISTENCE and COMMERCIAL farming systems with regard to goal, technology use and scale.
practice.memo:
• Subsistence: farmer grows crops/keeps livestock mainly to feed the FAMILY; rural, small plots, less technology
• Commercial: farmer produces to SELL for PROFIT; modern technology, large plots, may contribute to pollution

practice.q: Give FOUR reasons why a farmer provides shelter for farm animals.
practice.memo:
• Protection against predators
• Protection against extreme weather (heat/cold)
• Protection against wetting by rain
• Improves production performance

practice.q: Distinguish between: (i) Feedlot (ii) Battery cage system (iii) Broiler unit.
practice.memo:
• Feedlot: restricted area for large animals kept for maximum MEAT production
• Battery system: restricted cage system for LAYERS to maximise EGG production
• Broiler unit: restricted area for chickens raised for maximum MEAT production

practice.q: State FOUR basic layout requirements for a CHICKEN HOUSE.
practice.memo:
• Built on a slight slope for water run-off
• Dry bedding (straw, sawdust, wood shavings)
• Long side of building faces EAST (warm in winter, cool in summer)
• Adequate ventilation

practice.q: Define FLIGHT ZONE and identify the following positions in the flight zone diagram: (A) Handler (C) Blind spot (D) Balance point.
practice.memo:
• Flight zone: the distance an animal keeps between itself and a perceived threat/danger
• A = Handler: must stand OUTSIDE the flight zone edge to safely initiate movement
• C = Blind spot: area directly behind the animal where it cannot see and may KICK if approached
• D = Balance point: point at the shoulder controlling forward/backward movement of the animal

practice.q: Name FOUR abnormal behaviours of CATTLE under distress.
practice.memo: • Pawing / Rapid tail movement / Raised back fur / Snorting (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Name FOUR abnormal behaviours of PIGS under distress.
practice.memo: • Tail biting / Ear biting / Cannibalism / Belly nibbling (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Give FOUR reasons why farm animals need to be handled.
practice.memo: • Administering medication / Dehorning, castration, docking / Milking / Taking body weight, pregnancy testing, inseminating, transporting (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Describe the DESIGN FEATURES and PURPOSE of a CRUSH.
practice.memo:
• Purpose: used to restrain large animals for treatment, dehorning or inspection
• Design: high, strong, solid sides
• Design: wide and straight to provide a clear path
• Design: no sharp protruding objects

practice.q: State TWO design features of a loading RAMP.
practice.memo:
• Should NOT be slippery (non-slip surface/grooves)
• Should NOT be too steep (gentle slope)

practice.q: Name TWO INCORRECT handling methods of farm animals.
practice.memo:
• Throwing objects at animals
• Picking up chickens by their feathers (should be by feet/wings)

practice.q: Compile a table showing FIVE signs of GOOD HEALTH and FIVE corresponding signs of POOR HEALTH in farm animals.
practice.memo:
• Normal urine and faeces
• Normal milk production
• Animal walks normally
• Normal breathing rate
• Normal body temperature and pulse rate  *POOR HEALTH:*
• Discoloured urine; faeces too soft or too hard
• Low milk production
• Animal walks slowly/limps
• Forced/laboured breathing
• High body temperature; loss of appetite

practice.q: Explain how to TEST a farm animal's BODY TEMPERATURE: name the instrument, site of insertion and normal range. State TWO precautions.
practice.memo:
• Instrument: rectal thermometer
• Site of insertion: rectum
• Normal range: 37°C -- 40°C
• Precautions: tie thermometer with string so it isn't lost in the body; lubricate with sterile jelly

practice.q: Distinguish between INTRAVENOUS, INTRAMUSCULAR, SUBCUTANEOUS and INTRADERMAL routes of medicine administration.
practice.memo:
• Intravenous: into the VEIN --- fastest acting
• Intramuscular: into the MUSCLE
• Subcutaneous: under the SKIN
• Intradermal: between the LAYERS of skin

practice.q: Define each of the following: (i) Contagious disease (ii) Zoonotic disease (iii) Notifiable disease (iv) Epidemic disease.
practice.memo:
• Contagious disease: spreads through CONTACT
• Zoonotic disease: can spread from animals to humans and vice versa
• Notifiable disease: must be REPORTED to authorities upon observation
• Epidemic disease: affects a LARGE number of animals in an area

practice.q: Name the animals affected, FOUR symptoms, mode of transmission and treatment for FOOT AND MOUTH DISEASE.
practice.memo:
• Animals affected: ALL farm animals (cloven-hoofed)
• Symptoms: sores in mouth, tongue and hooves; high fever; excessive salivation (any 4 of these)
• Transmission: ingesting contaminated feed; contact with secretions/excretions of infected animals
• Treatment: vaccination; quarantine; slaughter and burn infected animals

practice.q: Name the animals affected, symptoms, mode of transmission and treatment for RABIES.
practice.memo:
• Animals affected: ALL farm animals (and humans)
• Symptoms: high fever, aggression, excessive salivation, paralysis of hindquarters
• Transmission: bite from an infected animal
• Treatment: vaccination; destroy infected animals and burn meat

practice.q: Name the animals affected, symptoms, mode of transmission and treatment for RIFT VALLEY FEVER.
practice.memo:
• Animals affected: ALL farm animals
• Symptoms: high fever; abortion
• Transmission: mosquito bite; handling infected meat/blood
• Treatment: vaccination; slaughter and burn infected animals

practice.q: Name the animals affected, symptoms, mode of transmission and treatment for NEW CASTLE DISEASE.
practice.memo:
• Animals affected: POULTRY
• Symptoms: twisted neck, green diarrhoea, rapid breathing
• Transmission: eating/drinking contaminated feed/water
• Treatment: vaccination; quarantine

practice.q: Name the pathogen type, animals affected, symptoms and treatment for MASTITIS.
practice.memo:
• Pathogen type: BACTERIA
• Animals affected: dairy cattle
• Symptoms: sores/swelling in udder; drop in milk production
• Treatment: intramammary antibiotic injection

practice.q: List FIVE measures for the PREVENTION of animal diseases.
practice.memo: • Vaccination / Quarantine / Movement permits / Dipping / Proper nutrition (any 5 × 1)

practice.q: Name FIVE economic implications of animal disease on a farm.
practice.memo: • Loss of production (milk, meat, eggs) / Cost of treatment / Loss of animals through death / Loss of markets/trade bans / Decline in livestock value (any 5 × 1)

practice.q: Distinguish between ENDOPARASITES and ECTOPARASITES.
practice.memo:
• Endoparasite: lives INSIDE the body of the host
• Ectoparasite: lives OUTSIDE the body on the skin of the host

practice.q: Describe the LIFE CYCLE of the LIVER FLUKE naming all stages and hosts. State THREE control methods.
practice.memo:
• Adult fluke in the liver of the PRIMARY host (e.g. cattle/sheep) lays eggs
• Eggs are released in FAECES onto pasture
• Eggs hatch into larvae (MIRACIDIUM) in water/grass
• Larvae enter the INTERMEDIATE host (snail)
• Larvae develop and leave the snail forming CYSTS (cercaria) on grass
• Cysts are eaten by the primary host and develop into adult flukes in the liver
• Control: destroy intermediate hosts (snails) / burn veld / rotational grazing (any 2 × 1)

practice.q: Distinguish between BLUE-TICK, BONT-LEGGED TICK and BONT-TICK regarding: number of hosts and disease caused.
practice.memo:
• Blue-tick: ONE host; causes Red-water and Gall-sickness (Anaplasmosis)
• Bont-legged tick: TWO hosts; causes Red-water
• Bont-tick: THREE hosts; causes Heart-water and Congo fever

practice.q: Describe FIVE ways to control BLOWFLY infestation in sheep.
practice.memo: • Tail docking / Crouching (removal of wool around tail and legs) / Wool shearing / Dipping / Treatment of wounds (any 5 × 1)

practice.q: Define UREA POISONING. Name FOUR symptoms and state the treatment.
practice.memo:
• Definition: occurs when urea intake is too high or poorly mixed --- excess nitrogen is converted to toxic AMMONIA
• Symptoms: bloating, rapid death, nervous excitement, incoordination (any 4 × 1)
• Treatment: administer VINEGAR Treatment included above

practice.q: For THORN APPLE: state FOUR symptoms, treatment and TWO prevention methods.
practice.memo:
• Symptoms: loss of appetite, extreme mouth dryness, slow growth, pupil dilation (any 4 × 1)
• Treatment: administer activated charcoal
• Prevention: remove infected plants from grazing area; practice rotational grazing (any 1)

practice.q: Compare ear tagging and branding as methods of livestock identification, giving ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage of each.
practice.memo:
• Ear tagging: Advantage — easy to read/apply, relatively low cost; Disadvantage — tags can be lost/torn out ✓✓
• Branding: Advantage — permanent, highly visible; Disadvantage — causes pain/stress to the animal and can damage the hide ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the importance of a vaccination programme on a livestock farm.
practice.memo:
• Vaccination stimulates the animal's immune system to produce antibodies against specific diseases ✓✓
• This provides protection/immunity, reducing the risk and severity of disease outbreaks ✓
• This reduces production losses, treatment costs and mortality, improving overall farm profitability ✓

practice.q: Describe THREE biosecurity measures a farmer can implement to reduce the risk of disease outbreaks on the farm.
practice.memo:
• Quarantine new animals before introducing them to the herd ✓✓
• Control access of visitors/vehicles onto the farm and provide foot baths/disinfection points ✓✓
• Maintain a regular vaccination and parasite control (dipping/deworming) programme ✓✓ (any 3 valid measures)

practice.q: Explain TWO reasons why farmers commonly castrate male animals not intended for breeding purposes.
practice.memo:
• Reduces aggressive and sexual behaviour, making animals easier and safer to manage ✓✓
• Improves meat quality/tenderness and prevents unwanted/uncontrolled breeding within the herd ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between extensive and intensive livestock production systems, giving an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Extensive: low stocking density, animals graze over large natural areas, lower input costs, e.g. beef cattle on veld/rangeland ✓✓
• Intensive: high stocking density, confined animals, formulated feed, higher input costs, e.g. a feedlot or broiler house ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the concept of carrying capacity and describe the consequences of overstocking land beyond its carrying capacity.
practice.memo:
• Carrying capacity: the maximum number of animals an area of land can sustainably support without long-term damage to the veld/pasture ✓
• Overstocking exceeds this capacity ✓
• Consequences: overgrazing, soil erosion/compaction, decline in pasture quality and quantity, ultimately reduced animal production ✓✓

practice.q: List FOUR of the 'Five Freedoms' of animal welfare.
practice.memo:
• Freedom from hunger and thirst ✓
• Freedom from discomfort ✓
• Freedom from pain, injury or disease ✓
• Freedom to express normal behaviour ✓
• Freedom from fear and distress ✓ (any 4)

mcq.q: The single, narrow, coiled tube that stores, matures, and transports sperm from the TESTES to the VAS DEFERENS is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Epididymis

mcq.q: During which stage of the oestrus cycle does OVULATION (release of egg from follicle) take place?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Met-oestrus

mcq.q: The organ in the female reproductive system that RETAINS AND NOURISHES the embryo/foetus during gestation is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Uterus

mcq.q: The FIRST MILK released after calving, which is rich in antibodies and immunoglobulins, is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Colostrum

mcq.q: An infertile cow with MASCULINISED BEHAVIOUR and non-functioning ovaries is called a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Freemartin

mcq.q: Which hormone is secreted by the CORPUS LUTEUM and functions to MAINTAIN PREGNANCY?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Progesterone

mcq.q: from the abdominal cavity into the scrotum is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cryptorchidism

mcq.q: During EMBRYO TRANSFER, the cow that CARRIES the embryo to full term is called the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Surrogate (Recipient)

mcq.q: Artificial Insemination (AI) involves ...
opts: Natural mating between a bull and cow
Collecting semen from a male and manually introducing it into the female reproductive tract using instruments
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. AI allows farmers to use genetically superior sires without the need to keep/transport breeding males.

mcq.q: A major ADVANTAGE of Artificial Insemination (AI) on a livestock farm is that it ...
opts: Increases the risk of spreading venereal disease compared to natural mating
Allows access to genetically superior sires, increases the number of females a single male's semen can impregnate, and reduces the cost/risk of keeping a breeding male
Requires no special training or equipment
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. AI dramatically increases the genetic impact of top-quality sires across many herds.

mcq.q: A DISADVANTAGE of Artificial Insemination (AI) is that it ...
opts: Requires skilled, trained personnel, specialised equipment, and accurate heat detection for successful timing
Eliminates the need for record keeping
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Success depends on correctly identifying when a female is in oestrus (heat) and proper technique/equipment.

mcq.q: Embryo Transfer (ET) is a reproductive technology in which ...
opts: A fertilised embryo from a genetically superior donor female is collected and implanted into the uterus of a recipient (surrogate) female
Hormones are used purely for synchronisation, with no embryo movement involved
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. ET allows a single valuable female to produce many more offspring per year than natural reproduction would allow.

mcq.q: Oestrus synchronisation, achieved through hormone treatment, allows farmers to ...
opts: Permanently sterilise a group of females
Bring a group of females into heat (oestrus) at approximately the same time, simplifying AI scheduling and management
Prevent all females from ever conceiving
Eliminate the need for pregnancy diagnosis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Synchronisation makes batch AI and calving/lambing seasons more predictable and manageable.

mcq.q: Pregnancy diagnosis in livestock (e.g. via rectal palpation or ultrasound) is important mainly because it allows farmers to ...
opts: Identify non-pregnant (open) females early so they can be re-bred or culled, improving overall reproductive efficiency
Replace the need for record keeping
Guarantee the sex of the offspring
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Early pregnancy diagnosis reduces the number of unproductive days in a breeding female's reproductive cycle.

mcq.q: Pure breeding (mating animals of the same breed) is mainly used to ...
opts: Maintain and improve the genetic uniformity/desirable characteristics of a specific breed
Reduce the value of breeding stock
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Pure breeding preserves recognised breed characteristics, useful for producing registered stud/breeding animals.

mcq.q: Crossbreeding (mating animals of two different breeds) is mainly used to ...
opts: Combine desirable traits of two breeds and take advantage of hybrid vigour (heterosis) in the offspring
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Crossbred offspring often outperform either pure parent breed for traits like growth rate and disease resistance.

mcq.q: Hybrid vigour (heterosis) refers to ...
opts: The decline in performance seen in inbred animals
The improved performance (e.g. growth, fertility, disease resistance) often observed in crossbred offspring compared to their purebred parents
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Heterosis arises because crossbred offspring inherit a wider range of beneficial alleles, often masking harmful recessive traits.

mcq.q: Inbreeding (mating closely related animals) carries the risk of ...
opts: Increasing the expression of harmful recessive alleles, leading to inbreeding depression (reduced fertility, vigour, and increased genetic defects)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Close relatives are more likely to share the same recessive alleles, increasing the chance of homozygous recessive (often harmful) offspring.

mcq.q: Selective breeding involves ...
opts: Deliberately choosing breeding animals with desirable traits to be parents of the next generation, gradually improving the herd/flock over time
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Selective breeding is the foundation of genetic improvement programmes in livestock.

mcq.q: The average gestation period of cattle is approximately ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cattle gestation averages around 283 days, similar in length to human pregnancy.

mcq.q: The average gestation period of sheep is approximately ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sheep have a notably shorter gestation period than cattle.

mcq.q: The average gestation period of a pig (sow) is approximately ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Pig gestation is commonly remembered as '3 months, 3 weeks and 3 days'.

mcq.q: Colostrum, the first milk produced after birth, is especially important for newborn livestock because it ...
opts: Is rich in antibodies (passive immunity), providing the newborn with vital disease protection before its own immune system matures
Should be avoided in the first 24 hours
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Newborn mammals are born with little/no immunity and rely on colostrum's antibodies for early protection against disease.

mcq.q: Dystocia refers to ...
opts: Difficult or obstructed birth/labour, often requiring human assistance
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dystocia can result from factors such as an oversized foetus, poor positioning, or a narrow birth canal, and may require veterinary intervention.

mcq.q: Weaning refers to the process of ...
opts: Gradually stopping a young animal's dependence on its mother's milk and transitioning it onto solid feed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Proper weaning management reduces stress on both the dam and offspring while supporting continued healthy growth.

practice.q: Name the NINE structures of the male reproductive system (A--I): Seminal vesicle, Prostate gland, Cowper's gland, Sperm duct, Urethra, Penis, Epididymis, Testis, Scrotum.
practice.memo:
• A = Seminal vesicle
• B = Prostate gland
• C = Cowper's gland
• D = Sperm duct (vas deferens)
• E = Urethra
• F = Penis
• G = Epididymis
• H = Testis
• I = Scrotum

practice.q: State the function of EACH of the following: (i) Scrotum (ii) Testis (iii) Testosterone (iv) Epididymis (v) Sperm duct (vi) Urethra (vii) Penis.
practice.memo:
• Scrotum: holds/protects testes; regulates temperature for sperm production
• Testis: produces sperm and testosterone
• Testosterone: stimulates sperm production and male secondary characteristics
• Epididymis: stores, matures and transports sperm
• Sperm duct: transports sperm to the urethra
• Urethra: transports urine and semen to the outside
• Penis: deposits semen into the female during mating

practice.q: Name the THREE specialised cells in the testes and state the function of each.
practice.memo:
• Sertoli cells: supply sperm cells with nutrients
• Leydig (interstitial) cells: produce testosterone
• Epithelial (germinal) cells: produce sperm via spermatogenesis

practice.q: Explain the process of SPERMATOGENESIS in four steps.
practice.memo:
• Step 1: Spermatogonium divides by MITOSIS to form primary spermatocytes (diploid)
• Step 2: Primary spermatocyte divides by MEIOSIS I to form secondary spermatocytes
• Step 3: Secondary spermatocyte divides by MEIOSIS II to form haploid spermatids
• Step 4: Spermatid differentiates and develops into a mature SPERM cell

practice.q: Name and state the function of EACH part of the sperm cell: Acrosome, Nucleus, Centriole, Mitochondria, Tail/Flagellum.
practice.memo:
• Acrosome: contains enzymes to digest the egg's outer membrane during fertilisation
• Nucleus: contains genetic material (DNA)
• Centriole: assists in cell division
• Mitochondria: supply energy (ATP) for sperm movement
• Tail/Flagellum: enables sperm movement (propulsion)

practice.q: Distinguish between SPERM CELL and SEMEN.
practice.memo:
• Sperm cell: the male GAMETE
• Semen: the FLUID containing sperm and secretions of accessory glands

practice.q: Distinguish between STERILITY and INFERTILITY in a bull. Name TWO factors causing EACH.
practice.memo:
• PERMANENT inability to reproduce; total loss of fertility; caused by congenital defects
• Causes: Hypoplasia (underdeveloped testes) / Cryptorchidism (undescended testes) (any 2 × 1)  *INFERTILITY:*
• TEMPORARY inability to reproduce; fertility NOT totally lost; CAN be corrected
• Causes: Obesity / Impotence / Poor nutrition (any 2 × 1)

practice.q: State the function of EACH of the following: (i) Ovary (ii) Oestrogen (iii) Progesterone (iv) Oviduct (v) Uterus (vi) Cervix (vii) Vagina.
practice.memo:
• Ovary: produces ova (eggs) and hormones oestrogen & progesterone
• Oestrogen: stimulates oestrus, endometrium thickening, female characteristics
• Progesterone: maintains pregnancy; prepares uterine wall for implantation
• Oviduct: catches egg at ovulation; site of fertilisation; transports egg to uterus
• Uterus: site of implantation and foetal development
• Cervix: secretes mucus to prevent infection; neutralises vaginal acidity
• Vagina: receives semen during mating; serves as birth canal

practice.q: Name TWO processes that occur in the OVARY and TWO processes that occur in the OVIDUCT.
practice.memo:
• Ovary: Oogenesis (egg formation) / Meiosis (any 2)
• Oviduct: Fertilisation / Transport of fertilised egg (any 2)

practice.q: Name and define TWO causes of STERILITY in cows.
practice.memo:
• Hypoplasia: underdevelopment of one/both ovaries
• Hermaphroditism: presence of both male and female reproductive organs

practice.q: Name and define THREE causes of INFERTILITY in cows.
practice.memo:
• Anoestrus: animal shows no signs of oestrus
• Nymphomania: cow is permanently on heat
• Repeat breeder: fails to conceive after several mating attempts

practice.q: Name FIVE female hormones controlling the oestrus cycle and state the function of EACH.
practice.memo:
• GnRH (hypothalamus): stimulates release of FSH and LH
• FSH (hypophysis): stimulates follicle development on the ovary
• Oestrogen (follicle): stimulates oestrus and endometrium development
• LH (hypophysis): triggers ovulation and corpus luteum formation
• Progesterone (corpus luteum): maintains pregnancy

practice.q: Name and describe the FOUR stages of the oestrus cycle in cows, including duration for each stage.
practice.memo:
• Pro-oestrus (≈3 days): follicle develops before oestrus
• Oestrus (≈18 hours): female receptive --- 'standing heat'
• Met-oestrus (≈4 days): ovulation occurs; corpus luteum begins to form
• Di-oestrus (≈16 days): corpus luteum fully developed, secretes progesterone; if not fertilised, it degenerates and the cycle restarts

practice.q: Name FOUR signs of oestrus (heat) in cows.
practice.memo: • Standing to be mounted by other cows / Restlessness/bellowing / Clear mucous discharge from vulva / Swollen red vulva, decreased appetite/milk (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Name and describe FOUR alternative methods of detecting a cow on heat.
practice.memo:
• Chin-ball marker: strapped on teaser animal's chin; leaves ink streak on mounted cow
• Chalking: chalk on cow's back/tail rubs off when mounted
• Pedometer: strapped to leg, detects increased movement
• Heat detector/Kamaar: glued to back, changes colour (white→red) under pressure

practice.q: Define SYNCHRONISATION OF OESTRUS and name TWO hormones used during this process.
practice.memo:
• Definition: bringing a group of females into oestrus at approximately the SAME time
• Hormones used: MGA (fed) and Prostaglandin (injected)

practice.q: Define ARTIFICIAL INSEMINATION (AI).
practice.memo: • AI: technique of collecting semen from a male and depositing it into the female's reproductive tract for fertilisation

practice.q: Name FOUR methods of semen collection.
practice.memo: • Electro-ejaculator / Artificial vagina / Collection from slaughtered animals / Manual stimulation (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: State the purpose of EACH of the following semen diluents: (i) Skimmed milk (ii) Egg yolk (iii) Glycerol (iv) Antibiotics.
practice.memo:
• Skimmed milk: controls/buffers pH
• Egg yolk: protects against temperature changes
• Glycerol: prevents freezing damage to sperm
• Antibiotics: prevent bacterial infection

practice.q: Describe MACROSCOPIC evaluation of semen colour: state what RED, BROWN and GREY colour indicate.
practice.memo:
• Red: indicates FRESH blood contamination
• Brown: indicates INFECTION
• Grey: indicates OLD blood contamination

practice.q: List THREE advantages and TWO disadvantages of Artificial Insemination.
practice.memo:
• Advantages (any 3): genetic material of a superior bull used widely; semen usable after the male's death; reduced disease transmission; cheaper than keeping a bull; semen transportable
• Disadvantages (any 2): requires skilled technician; equipment needed; accurate timing required

practice.q: List the FIVE steps of EMBRYO TRANSFER in the correct order.
practice.memo:
• 1. Synchronisation of donor and recipient
• 2. Superovulation of donor
• 3. AI of donor
• 4. Embryo flushing from donor
• 5. Embryo transfer to recipient

practice.q: Give TWO advantages and TWO disadvantages of EMBRYO TRANSFER.
practice.memo:
• Advantages (any 2): more offspring from a genetically superior donor; donor continues producing while recipient carries the calf
• Disadvantages (any 2): expensive; requires skilled technician; not always successful

practice.q: Define CLONING and name the TWO types of cloning, explaining each.
practice.memo:
• Cloning: producing an offspring GENETICALLY IDENTICAL to the original organism
• Reproductive cloning: results in a whole organism; cloned embryo placed in a surrogate uterus
• Therapeutic cloning: used to produce organs/tissues/cells from stem cells for medical purposes --- NOT to create offspring

practice.q: Define PREGNANCY (gestation) and state the gestation period of a cow.
practice.memo: • Pregnancy: the period from fertilisation to birth. Cow gestation = 280 days

practice.q: Name and describe the THREE extra-embryonic membranes that cover the embryo.
practice.memo:
• Chorion: outer membrane attached to the uterus wall
• Amnion: inner membrane enclosing amniotic fluid; connected to embryo via umbilical cord
• Allantois: arises from urinary system; collects foetal urine

practice.q: Name and describe the THREE primordial germ layers of an embryo.
practice.memo:
• Ectoderm: outer layer --- forms nervous system & skin
• Mesoderm: middle layer --- forms muscles, bones, urogenital organs
• Endoderm: inner layer --- forms digestive system & internal organs

practice.q: Define ABORTION and give THREE causes.
practice.memo:
• Abortion: termination of pregnancy before normal parturition
• Causes (any 3): disease/infection, nutritional deficiency, hormonal imbalance, physical injury, genetic defects, stress

practice.q: Describe MUMMIFICATION, RESORPTION, MACERATION and HYDRO-FOETUS.
practice.memo:
• Mummification: foetus dies after skin/skeleton form; amniotic fluid and placenta dry out
• Resorption: pregnancy terminates without visible signs; foetus is absorbed
• Maceration: decay of soft tissue, leaving only bones; risk of bacterial infection
• Hydro-foetus: excessive amniotic fluid collects around/in the foetus

practice.q: Name FOUR signs of PARTURITION in a cow.
practice.memo: • Cow isolates herself / Udder swells / Vulva swells and relaxes / Restlessness, frequent lying down (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Name and describe the THREE stages of PARTURITION.
practice.memo:
• Preparatory stage: cervix dilates, uterine muscles contract
• Ejection stage: amniotic sac appears; calf born head and forelegs first
• Expulsion of placenta: placenta and umbilical cord expelled

practice.q: Name FOUR causes of DYSTOCIA and describe TWO indigenous treatments.
practice.memo:
• Causes: deviation of head, retention of forelegs, hydrocephalus, twins, posterior presentation, large foetus (any 4 × 1)
• Indigenous treatments: apply oils/grease to ease delivery; apply vinegar; contact traditional healer (any 2 × 1)

practice.q: Define LACTATION and state its duration in a dairy cow.
practice.memo: • Lactation: milk production process; duration ≈ 305 days

practice.q: Name the hormones involved in the MILKING REFLEX and state the function of each.
practice.memo:
• Prolactin: stimulates milk PRODUCTION
• Oxytocin: stimulates milk RELEASE (let-down)
• Oestrogen (high levels): inhibits/stops milk production

practice.q: List FIVE characteristics of COLOSTRUM.
practice.memo: • First milk after calving (within ~3 days) / Yellower colour / Higher in protein, fats, lactose, minerals / Increases disease resistance (antibodies) / Rich in immunoglobulins (any 5 × 1)

practice.q: The table below shows a cow's milk yield and butterfat content over a 40-week lactation period: --------------------- ------------------------ ------------------------ **WEEKS** **MILK YIELD (litres)** **MILK BUTTERFAT CONTENT (%)** 1 30 5 10 30 10 20 20 20 30 10 30 40 5 30 --------------------- ------------------------ ------------------------ **(a)** Present the information in a LINE GRAPH with key, labels and title.
practice.memo:
• Graph (6): correct x-axis (Weeks) and y-axis (Litres / %); two clearly labelled lines with a key/legend; accurate plotting of all 5 data points for each variable; suitable title
• Relationship (4): as MILK PRODUCTION increases, BUTTERFAT content DECREASES; as milk production declines after peak (week 1--10), butterfat content steadily INCREASES toward the end of lactation (week 40)

practice.q: Discuss TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of using Artificial Insemination (AI) compared to natural mating on a commercial farm.
practice.memo:
Advantages (any 2):
• Access to genetically superior sires without owning/transporting a breeding male ✓✓
• A single ejaculate can be used to inseminate many females, increasing genetic spread ✓✓
• Reduces the risk of spreading venereal/reproductive diseases compared to natural mating ✓✓
Disadvantage:
• Requires skilled labour, equipment, and accurate heat detection/timing; can be costly ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the difference between pure breeding and crossbreeding, and describe ONE advantage of each.
practice.memo:
• Pure breeding: mating animals of the SAME breed; maintains breed standards/uniform characteristics, used for stud breeding ✓✓✓
• Crossbreeding: mating animals of DIFFERENT breeds; takes advantage of hybrid vigour (heterosis), combining desirable traits of both breeds, often improving production traits ✓✓✓

practice.q: Tabulate the approximate gestation period of cattle, sheep and pigs.
practice.memo:
• Cattle — approximately 283 days ✓
• Sheep — approximately 147–150 days ✓
• Pigs — approximately 114 days ✓

practice.q: Explain the importance of colostrum intake for a newborn calf within the first 24 hours of life.
practice.memo:
• Colostrum is rich in antibodies (immunoglobulins) ✓
• Newborn calves are born with little to no natural immunity of their own ✓
• Antibodies in colostrum can only be absorbed efficiently through the calf's gut wall within roughly the first 24 hours of life ✓
• This passive immunity protects the calf from disease until its own immune system develops ✓

practice.q: Explain why inbreeding (mating closely related animals) is generally avoided in commercial livestock breeding programmes.
practice.memo:
• Closely related animals are more likely to carry the same recessive (often harmful) alleles ✓
• Inbreeding increases the chance of offspring being homozygous recessive for these harmful alleles, expressing genetic defects ✓
• This leads to 'inbreeding depression' — reduced fertility, vigour and overall productivity ✓

mcq.q: The process involving the MOVEMENT of a product from the farm to the consumer is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Marketing

mcq.q: The LAW OF DEMAND states that ...
opts: Higher price leads to higher demand
Higher price leads to lower demand
Higher price leads to higher supply
Lower price leads to lower supply
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Higher price leads to lower demand

mcq.q: MARKET EQUILIBRIUM is the point where ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Demand and supply are equal

mcq.q: A price floor (minimum price set by government/regulator) is typically set ...
opts: Below equilibrium to prevent prices rising
Above equilibrium to prevent prices falling too low
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Above equilibrium to prevent prices falling too low

mcq.q: segment of a market is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Niche marketing

mcq.q: Two or more independent farms working together to meet common needs form an ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Agricultural cooperative

mcq.q: Selling livestock to the HIGHEST BIDDER in an open-market setting is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Stock auction

mcq.q: The marketing policy instrument focused on \'HOW to convince consumers to buy the product\' is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Promotion

mcq.q: When the price of petrol increases and people STILL buy it because there is no substitute, this shows ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Price inelasticity of demand

mcq.q: What is a disadvantage of selling produce through a fresh produce/auction market?
opts: Middlemen take a cut of profit
The farmer must transport products to a distant market
Volume sold may be small and prices may fluctuate
Farmers lose direct contact with consumers
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Volume sold may be small and prices may fluctuate

mcq.q: how to achieve them
opts: A short list of annual expenses
A document describing how a business will operate, its goals, and
A document showing only income and expenses
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A document describing how a business will operate, its goals, and

mcq.q: The FIRST phase of entrepreneurship is to ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Identify an opportunity

mcq.q: What is the purpose of conducting a SWOT analysis on a farm business?
opts: Evaluate a business by identifying internal and external factors
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Evaluate a business by identifying internal and external factors

mcq.q: In a SWOT analysis, a competitor going bankrupt would be classified as a/an ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Opportunity

mcq.q: Increased competition and increased interest rates are examples of which SWOT component?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Threats

mcq.q: A sole proprietorship is a form of business ownership characterised by ...
opts: A single owner who has full control but also unlimited personal liability for the business's debts
Shares traded on a stock exchange
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sole proprietorships are simple and cheap to establish but expose the owner's personal assets to business risk.

mcq.q: A partnership, as a form of business ownership, is characterised by ...
opts: Two or more people who jointly own and manage the business, sharing profits, losses and liability according to an agreement
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Partnerships allow shared capital, skills and decision-making, but partners often share unlimited liability too.

mcq.q: A company (Pty Ltd / Ltd), as a form of business ownership, generally offers owners (shareholders) the advantage of ...
opts: Unlimited personal liability for company debts
Limited liability, meaning shareholders' personal assets are generally protected if the business fails, as it is a separate legal entity
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A company is a separate legal entity, so shareholders typically only risk the amount they invested.

mcq.q: A cooperative is a form of business ownership where ...
opts: A single individual controls all decisions and profits
Members (often farmers) jointly own and democratically control the enterprise, sharing benefits/profits according to their participation
Only the government can be a member
Profits are distributed only to outside investors
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Agricultural cooperatives allow farmers to pool resources for buying inputs, marketing produce, or accessing services more efficiently/cheaply.

mcq.q: Gross income, in farm financial terms, refers to ...
opts: The total income/revenue generated from selling farm products, before any expenses are subtracted
Only the profit retained by the owner
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Gross income is the starting point before costs are deducted to determine net profit.

mcq.q: Net profit (net income) is calculated as ...
opts: Gross income − total expenses (fixed and variable costs)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Net profit reflects the actual financial gain after all costs of production have been deducted from income.

mcq.q: Fixed costs in farming are costs that ...
opts: Vary directly with the level of production
Remain relatively constant regardless of the level of production, e.g. land rent, insurance, fixed loan repayments
Are only incurred once production exceeds a certain level
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Fixed costs must be paid whether or not any product is produced/sold.

mcq.q: Variable costs in farming are costs that ...
opts: Remain the same no matter how much is produced
Change in direct proportion to the level of production, e.g. feed, seed, fertiliser, fuel
Are paid only once a year regardless of output
Never need to be budgeted for
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Variable costs rise as production increases and fall as production decreases.

mcq.q: A break-even point in a farming enterprise is the point at which ...
opts: Total income exceeds total costs significantly
Total income exactly equals total costs, resulting in neither a profit nor a loss
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Beyond the break-even point, additional sales generate profit; below it, the enterprise operates at a loss.

mcq.q: A cash flow statement/budget is a financial record that shows ...
opts: Only the value of farm assets
The actual or projected inflows and outflows of cash over a specific period, helping a farmer plan for cash shortages
The market price of farm produce only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cash flow planning helps ensure a farmer has enough cash on hand to meet expenses at the right times.

mcq.q: A balance sheet provides a summary of a farm business's ...
opts: Income and expenses over a year only
Assets, liabilities and owner's equity (net worth) at a specific point in time
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A balance sheet shows financial position (what is owned vs owed) at a given date.

mcq.q: A farming enterprise's net worth (owner's equity) is calculated as ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Net worth reflects what the owner would retain after all debts (liabilities) are settled using the farm's assets.

mcq.q: Demand, in economic terms, refers to ...
opts: The total quantity of a product available for sale
The quantity of a product that consumers are willing and able to buy at a given price, over a given period
Only the price of a product
The cost of producing a product
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Demand reflects consumer willingness/ability to purchase, which is influenced strongly by price.

mcq.q: Supply, in economic terms, refers to ...
opts: Consumer willingness to purchase a product
The quantity of a product that producers are willing and able to sell at a given price, over a given period
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Supply reflects producer behaviour, which generally increases as price increases (all else being equal).

mcq.q: According to the law of demand, as the price of a product increases (all else remaining equal), the quantity demanded generally ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Higher prices typically reduce the quantity consumers are willing/able to purchase, illustrated by a downward-sloping demand curve.

mcq.q: According to the law of supply, as the price of a product increases (all else remaining equal), the quantity supplied generally ...
opts: Increases, as higher prices incentivise producers to supply more
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Higher prices make production more profitable, encouraging producers to supply more, shown by an upward-sloping supply curve.

mcq.q: Market equilibrium occurs at the price where ...
opts: Supply is much greater than demand
The quantity supplied equals the quantity demanded
Demand is much greater than supply
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. At equilibrium, there is no surplus or shortage, and the market price tends to stabilise at this point.

mcq.q: Direct marketing (e.g. selling produce directly to consumers at a farm stall or farmers' market) has the advantage of ...
opts: Allowing the farmer to receive a larger share of the final selling price by cutting out intermediaries
Guaranteeing the lowest possible price for the farmer
Eliminating the need for any marketing effort
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Direct marketing reduces the number of intermediaries taking a margin, increasing the farmer's potential profit per unit sold.

mcq.q: Selling produce through a commodity exchange/auction system (e.g. a fresh produce market) has the advantage of ...
opts: Guaranteed fixed prices regardless of supply and demand
Price discovery through competitive bidding, often reaching a larger pool of buyers
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Auction-based systems allow market forces (supply and demand on the day) to determine a competitive price.

mcq.q: A cooperative marketing channel allows individual farmers to ...
opts: Compete against each other for the same buyers
Pool their produce/resources together, gaining greater bargaining power and potentially better prices and reduced costs (e.g. shared transport)
Avoid selling any produce at all
Bypass the need for quality standards
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cooperative marketing increases farmers' collective negotiating power compared to selling individually.

mcq.q: A SWOT analysis used in farm business planning examines a business's ...
opts: Supply, Wholesale price, Output and Trade
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. SWOT analysis helps a farmer assess internal factors (strengths/weaknesses) and external factors (opportunities/threats) when planning strategy.

mcq.q: A business/farm plan is important mainly because it ...
opts: Is only required when applying for government grants
Provides a structured outline of goals, strategies, resources needed and financial projections, guiding decision-making and helping secure financing
Replaces the need for record keeping
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A well-prepared business plan helps a farmer plan resource use and is often required by banks/lenders for finance applications.

practice.q: Define the term ENTREPRENEURSHIP.
practice.memo: • The PROCESS of identifying a business opportunity, gathering resources and taking the RISK to start and manage a business in order to make a PROFIT

practice.q: Define the term ENTREPRENEUR.
practice.memo: • A person who identifies a business opportunity and is willing to take the RISK of starting and running a business for profit

practice.q: Distinguish between an ENTREPRENEUR and a MANAGER (give TWO differences).
practice.memo:
• Entrepreneur: OWNS the business; takes financial RISK; innovative/creates new ideas
• Manager: is EMPLOYED to run the business; does NOT take the financial risk; implements existing plans

practice.q: List the FIVE phases of entrepreneurship in the correct order.
practice.memo:
• 1. Identify an opportunity
• 2. Develop a business plan
• 3. Determine resources required
• 4. Manage the business
• 5. Harvest the business (exit strategy/expansion)

practice.q: Name FOUR characteristics of a successful agricultural entrepreneur.
practice.memo: • Self-confidence / Hard-working / Risk-taking / Innovative / Good decision-making / Persistent (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Define the term BUSINESS PLAN.
practice.memo: • A WRITTEN DOCUMENT that describes the nature of the business, its goals/objectives and HOW the business intends to achieve those goals

practice.q: Give FOUR reasons why a farmer would draw up a business plan.
