practice.memo: • To obtain FINANCE/loans from banks / To set clear GOALS and objectives / To attract investors or partners / To MONITOR progress against targets (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Name FOUR problems that may be encountered when drawing up a business plan.
practice.memo: • Lack of information/research / Lack of financial/accounting skills / Unclear goals / Inability to predict future market conditions (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Give FOUR solutions to the problems mentioned in QUESTION 2.2.3.
practice.memo: • Conduct thorough market research / Consult a financial advisor/accountant / Set SMART (clear, measurable) goals / Use realistic, conservative projections (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Testing the FEASIBILITY of a business idea means determining whether it will succeed. List the THREE questions a farmer must ask when testing feasibility.
practice.memo:
• Is there a DEMAND for the product/service?
• Are the necessary RESOURCES (capital, labour, land) available?
• Will the business be PROFITABLE/sustainable in the long run?

practice.q: Write down the FULL WORDS represented by the acronym SWOT.
practice.memo:
• S = Strengths
• W = Weaknesses
• O = Opportunities
• T = Threats

practice.q: Explain the difference between INTERNAL and EXTERNAL factors in a SWOT analysis.
practice.memo:
• Internal factors (Strengths & Weaknesses): factors WITHIN the business that can be CONTROLLED by the business itself
• External factors (Opportunities & Threats): factors OUTSIDE the business that CANNOT be controlled by the business

practice.q: A farmer is considering starting a poultry enterprise. Conduct a SWOT analysis by giving TWO examples for EACH of the four components. **(a)** Strengths
practice.memo:
• Good location near markets / Skilled labour available / High demand for chicken meat/eggs  *Weaknesses (any 2):*
• Limited capital/finance / Lack of experience / Outdated equipment  *Opportunities (any 2):*
• Growing market for organic/free-range products / Government support programmes for new farmers  *Threats (any 2):*
• Disease outbreaks (e.g. Newcastle disease, Avian flu) / Rising feed costs / Increased competition from established producers

practice.q: Define MARKETING.
practice.memo: • Marketing is the PROCESS of moving a product/service from the PRODUCER (farm) to the CONSUMER, including all activities involved in identifying, anticipating and satisfying consumer needs profitably

practice.q: Name and briefly describe the FOUR marketing policy instruments (4Ps).
practice.memo:
• Product: deciding WHAT to produce --- quality, packaging, branding to meet consumer needs
• Price: deciding WHAT PRICE to charge --- considering production costs, competitor prices and consumer affordability
• Place (Placement): deciding HOW/WHERE the product will reach the consumer --- distribution channels, location
• Promotion: deciding HOW to communicate with and CONVINCE consumers to buy --- advertising, sales promotions, branding

practice.q: State the LAW OF DEMAND and the LAW OF SUPPLY.
practice.memo:
• Law of Demand: as the PRICE of a product INCREASES, the QUANTITY DEMANDED DECREASES (and vice versa), all other factors remaining constant --- an INVERSE relationship
• Law of Supply: as the PRICE of a product INCREASES, the QUANTITY SUPPLIED also INCREASES (and vice versa), all other factors remaining constant --- a DIRECT relationship

practice.q: Define MARKET EQUILIBRIUM.
practice.memo: • Market equilibrium: the point where the QUANTITY DEMANDED equals the QUANTITY SUPPLIED at a given price --- the market is in BALANCE with no shortage or surplus

• (a) Equilibrium price = R3/box; Equilibrium quantity = 7 000 boxes (where quantity demanded = quantity supplied)
• (b) At R5/box, quantity supplied (12 000)  quantity demanded (2 000), causing an OVERSUPPLY/SURPLUS --- prices would fall back toward equilibrium as producers compete to sell excess stock

practice.q: Distinguish between a FLOOR PRICE and a CEILING PRICE, including the reason government sets each.
practice.memo:
• Floor price: a MINIMUM price set by the government ABOVE the equilibrium price, to ensure farmers receive a fair/reasonable income and are protected from prices falling too low
• Ceiling price: a MAXIMUM price set by the government BELOW the equilibrium price, to protect CONSUMERS from prices that are too high (e.g. for basic foodstuffs)

practice.q: Name THREE free marketing channels and briefly describe EACH.
practice.memo:
• Farm-gate marketing: the farmer sells products DIRECTLY to consumers at the farm, cutting out middlemen
• Fresh produce market: farmers transport products to a central market where they are sold to the highest bidder/wholesalers
• Stock auction: livestock are sold to the HIGHEST BIDDER at an organised auction

practice.q: Compare a sole proprietorship and a company (Pty Ltd) in terms of liability of the owner(s).
practice.memo:
• Sole proprietorship: the owner has UNLIMITED liability — personal assets can be used to pay off business debts ✓✓
• Company (Pty Ltd): shareholders have LIMITED liability — they are generally only liable up to the amount they invested, as the company is a separate legal entity ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between fixed costs and variable costs in a farming enterprise, giving ONE example of each.
practice.memo:
• Fixed costs: remain constant regardless of production level, e.g. land rent/insurance ✓✓
• Variable costs: change in proportion to production level, e.g. feed or fertiliser cost ✓✓

practice.q: A farmer has a gross income of R450 000 and total expenses (fixed + variable costs) of R310 000 for the year. Calculate the farmer's net profit.
practice.memo:
• Net profit = Gross income − Total expenses ✓
• = R450 000 − R310 000 ✓
• = R140 000 ✓

practice.q: Explain the relationship between price and quantity demanded, according to the law of demand.
practice.memo:
• As price increases, quantity demanded generally decreases (and vice versa), all else being equal ✓✓
• This is because consumers are less willing/able to purchase a product at a higher price, and may switch to substitute products ✓

practice.q: Discuss TWO advantages of direct marketing for a farmer compared to selling through a middleman.
practice.memo:
• The farmer receives a larger share/percentage of the final selling price, since no middleman commission is deducted ✓✓
• The farmer can build direct relationships with consumers and receive immediate feedback on product quality/preferences ✓✓ (any 2 valid advantages)

mcq.q: Which of the following is a characteristic of LAND as a production factor?
opts: It is mobile and can be relocated
It depreciates in value over time
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Its value appreciates over time

mcq.q: The law stating that increased amounts of an input do NOT give proportional increases in output is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Law of diminishing return

mcq.q: Labour employed for specific duties during a particular period of the year (e.g. harvest time) is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Seasonal labour

mcq.q: Capital invested in short-term assets used to pay operational expenses (seeds, fertilizer, fuel, wages) is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Working/floating capital

mcq.q: The Act regulating working hours, overtime, minimum wages, leave and termination conditions is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Basic Conditions of Employment Act

mcq.q: WORTH of a farming business is a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Balance sheet

mcq.q: The Act ensuring workers are trained on machinery, supplied protective clothing and a safe working environment is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Occupational Health and Safety Act

mcq.q: one document is called a ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Whole farm budget

mcq.q: The management function involving measuring and verifying results against planned objectives is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Control

mcq.q: Credit used to expand FIXED ASSETS (buildings, land, dams) with a repayment period of OVER 10 years is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Long-term credit

mcq.q: How is net profit (net income) calculated?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Total income − Total expenditure

mcq.q: Medium-term capital is typically used to purchase ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Tractors and breeding livestock

mcq.q: Which of the following is an example of UNSKILLED labour on a farm?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. General hand/weeder

mcq.q: The NET WORTH of a business is calculated as ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Total assets − Total liabilities

mcq.q: Strategic management in farming primarily involves ...
opts: Long-term planning to achieve farm goals and respond to changes
Hiring and firing of staff only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Long-term planning to achieve farm goals and respond to changes

mcq.q: The FOUR main factors of production in agriculture are generally identified as ...
opts: Soil, Water, Sun and Air only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. These four broad categories of resources are combined to produce agricultural goods and services.

mcq.q: Land, as a factor of production, refers to ...
opts: Only the soil used for crop production
All natural resources used in production, including soil, water, minerals and climate
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Land as a factor of production is a broad term covering all natural resources, not just the physical ground.

mcq.q: Capital, as a factor of production, refers to ...
opts: Only physical cash held by the farmer
Man-made resources used in production, such as machinery, buildings, tools and money invested in the business
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Capital includes both fixed capital (e.g. tractors, buildings) and working/operating capital (cash for running expenses).

mcq.q: Fixed capital differs from working (operating) capital in that fixed capital ...
opts: Is used up/consumed within one production cycle
Consists of durable assets used repeatedly over several production cycles, e.g. machinery, buildings, fencing
Has no value to the business
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Fixed capital items have a long working life, while working capital (e.g. cash for seed, feed) is used up each season/cycle.

mcq.q: Labour, as a factor of production, includes ...
opts: All human effort (physical and mental) contributed to the production process, both skilled and unskilled
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Labour covers the full range of human input, from manual farm work to skilled management decisions.

mcq.q: Entrepreneurship/management, as a factor of production, refers to the ability to ...
opts: Organise and combine the other factors of production (land, labour, capital), take risks, and make decisions to run a profitable enterprise
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The entrepreneur/manager coordinates resources and bears the risk of the business, aiming to generate a profit.

mcq.q: Productivity of land can be improved through practices such as ...
opts: Soil conservation, crop rotation, and appropriate fertilisation/irrigation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sustainable management practices maintain or improve soil fertility and productive capacity over the long term.

mcq.q: Labour productivity can be improved through ...
opts: Appropriate training, fair remuneration, motivation, and the use of suitable mechanisation/equipment
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Well-trained, motivated and properly equipped workers tend to be significantly more productive.

mcq.q: Mechanisation (the use of machinery) on a farm primarily aims to ...
opts: Increase the amount of manual labour required
Increase labour efficiency/productivity and reduce the time and physical effort needed to complete farming tasks
Replace the need for any management
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mechanisation substitutes capital (machinery) for labour, often increasing the scale and efficiency of operations.

mcq.q: Diminishing returns, in agricultural production, refers to the principle that ...
opts: Increasing one input indefinitely always results in proportionally increasing output
Beyond a certain point, adding more of one input (e.g. fertiliser) while keeping other inputs constant results in progressively smaller increases in output
Output always decreases immediately with any input increase
Inputs and outputs have no relationship
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This economic principle explains why there is an optimal input level beyond which extra input becomes less cost-effective.

mcq.q: Sustainable use of land as a production factor means ...
opts: Maximising short-term output regardless of long-term consequences
Using natural resources in a way that meets current production needs without compromising the land's productive capacity for future generations
Avoiding the use of any fertiliser or technology
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sustainable land use balances current productivity with the conservation of soil, water and other resources for the future.

mcq.q: A shortage of skilled agricultural labour in a region can be addressed through ...
opts: Investment in training/education programmes, fair wages to attract and retain workers, and appropriate use of mechanisation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A combination of training, incentives and technology can help overcome skilled labour shortages.

practice.q: Define LAND as a production factor in agriculture.
practice.memo: • Land is a NATURAL production factor that provides the SPACE and natural resources (soil, water, minerals) needed for agricultural production

practice.q: Name the FOUR production factors in agriculture.
practice.memo: • Land, Labour, Capital, Management (Entrepreneurship)

practice.q: Give FIVE economic characteristics of land that distinguish it from other production factors.
practice.memo: • Immobile (cannot be moved) / APPRECIATES in value over time / Limited in supply (fixed quantity) / Indestructible (cannot be destroyed, only degraded) / Heterogeneous (varies in quality/fertility) (any 5 × 1)

practice.q: State the LAW OF DIMINISHING RETURNS and explain its relevance to crop production.
practice.memo:
• Statement: as MORE units of a variable input (e.g. fertilizer) are added to a FIXED resource (land), the additional output (marginal return) EVENTUALLY DECREASES
• Relevance: helps a farmer determine the OPTIMUM level of fertilizer/input to apply for maximum profit without wasting resources on inputs beyond the point of diminishing returns

practice.q: Define LABOUR as a production factor.
practice.memo: • Labour is the HUMAN EFFORT (physical and mental) applied to production in order to generate income

practice.q: Distinguish between SKILLED, SEMI-SKILLED and UNSKILLED labour, giving ONE example of each.
practice.memo:
• Skilled labour: workers with FORMAL TRAINING/qualifications, e.g. a veterinarian or farm manager
• Semi-skilled labour: workers with SOME training/experience, e.g. a tractor driver
• Unskilled labour: workers with NO formal training, performing manual tasks, e.g. a general farm hand/weeder

practice.q: Classify and describe: PERMANENT, CASUAL and SEASONAL labour.
practice.memo:
• Permanent labour: employed on a LONG-TERM/full-time basis with a fixed monthly wage and benefits
• Casual labour: employed for SHORT, irregular periods, often paid daily
• Seasonal labour: employed for SPECIFIC periods of the year (e.g. harvest season)

practice.q: Name FOUR problems related to labour in agricultural production.
practice.memo: • High labour turnover / Strikes and labour disputes / Shortage of skilled labour / Rising minimum wage costs (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Match each labour legislation Act to what it regulates/ensures: ---------------------------- ------------------------------------------ **ACT** **WHAT IT REGULATES/ENSURES** Basic Conditions of Employment Act Labour Relations Act Occupational Health and Safety Act Skills Development Act ---------------------------- ------------------------------------------
practice.memo:
• Basic Conditions of Employment Act: regulates working hours, overtime, leave, minimum wages and termination conditions
• Labour Relations Act: governs the relationship between employer and employee, including dispute resolution, unions and strikes
• Occupational Health and Safety Act: ensures a SAFE working environment, training on machinery and provision of protective clothing
• Skills Development Act: promotes TRAINING and skills development of workers

practice.q: Define CAPITAL as a production factor.
practice.memo: • Capital refers to all the FINANCIAL RESOURCES and ASSETS (money, machinery, buildings, livestock) used in the production process

practice.q: Distinguish between FIXED, MEDIUM-TERM and WORKING (floating) capital. Give an example and repayment period for each.
practice.memo:
• Fixed capital: long-term assets, e.g. land, buildings, dams; repayment period OVER 10 years
• Medium-term capital: assets such as tractors, implements, breeding livestock; repayment period 2--10 years
• Working/floating capital: short-term operational expenses, e.g. seeds, fertilizer, wages, fuel; repayment period UNDER 2 years/1 production cycle

practice.q: Distinguish between a GRANT and CREDIT as sources of finance.
practice.memo:
• Grant: money given to a farmer that does NOT need to be repaid
• Credit: money BORROWED from a financial institution that MUST be repaid with interest

practice.q: Name THREE types of credit available to farmers, and give an example of what each is used for.
practice.memo:
• Short-term credit: used for operational expenses such as seeds and fertilizer, repaid within 1 year
• Medium-term credit: used to purchase equipment/breeding stock, repaid over 2--10 years
• Long-term credit: used to purchase land or build infrastructure, repaid over more than 10 years

practice.q: Give FOUR reasons why a farmer should keep financial records.
practice.memo: • To calculate profit/loss / To apply for loans (proof of income) / To make informed management decisions / For tax purposes (any 4 × 1)

practice.q: Distinguish between a BALANCE SHEET and an INCOME STATEMENT.
practice.memo:
• Balance sheet: shows the financial POSITION of a business at a SPECIFIC POINT in time (assets, liabilities, net worth)
• Income statement: shows the financial PERFORMANCE of a business over a PERIOD of time (income, expenditure, profit/loss)

practice.q: Study the following income statement and answer the questions: -------------------------------------- -------------------------------- **INCOME** **AMOUNT (R)** Maize sales 320 000 Soya sales 95 000 TOTAL INCOME 415 000 EXPENDITURE AMOUNT (R) Seeds 48 000 Fertilizer & chemicals 76 000 Wages 88 000 Fuel & maintenance 52 000 TOTAL EXPENDITURE 264 000 -------------------------------------- -------------------------------- **(a)** Calculate the NET PROFIT for this farming business. Show the formula used.
practice.memo:
• Net Profit = Total Income − Total Expenditure = R415 000 − R264 000 = R151 000
• If expenditure was R430 000: Total Expenditure (R430 000)  Total Income (R415 000) → this results in a NET LOSS of R15 000, meaning the business spent more than it earned

practice.q: Name the FOUR main factors of production and give ONE agricultural example of each.
practice.memo:
• Land — e.g. soil/grazing land used for crop or livestock production ✓✓
• Labour — e.g. farm workers who plant, harvest or care for livestock ✓✓
• Capital — e.g. a tractor, irrigation system or farm buildings ✓✓
• Entrepreneurship/Management — e.g. the farmer/manager who plans and makes decisions, taking financial risk ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between fixed capital and working (operating) capital, giving an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Fixed capital: durable assets used repeatedly over several years/production cycles, e.g. tractors, farm buildings, fencing ✓✓
• Working capital: funds used up within a single production cycle for day-to-day expenses, e.g. money spent on seed, fertiliser, feed or fuel ✓✓

practice.q: Discuss THREE ways a farmer can improve the productivity of farm labour.
practice.memo:
• Provide appropriate training/skills development to workers ✓✓
• Ensure fair remuneration and good working conditions to motivate workers ✓✓
• Provide suitable tools/machinery (mechanisation) to make tasks more efficient ✓✓ (any 3 valid points)

practice.q: Explain the principle of diminishing returns, using fertiliser application as an example.
practice.memo:
• As increasing amounts of fertiliser are applied to a fixed area of land (with other inputs unchanged), crop yield initially increases ✓✓
• However, beyond an optimal point, each additional unit of fertiliser produces progressively smaller increases in yield ✓
• Eventually, additional fertiliser may even decrease yield (over-fertilisation), making further application uneconomical ✓

mcq.q: The scientist regarded as the father of genetics, who conducted experiments on pea plants, is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Gregor Mendel

mcq.q: An organism with TWO DIFFERENT alleles for a trait (e.g. Bb) is described as ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Heterozygous

mcq.q: WHITE cow (bb). The genotype of ALL F1 offspring will be ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Bb

mcq.q: flower producing offspring that are ALL PINK shows ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Incomplete dominance

mcq.q: cow that produces ROAN offspring shows ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Co-dominance

mcq.q: Characteristics such as MILK YIELD, GROWTH RATE and EGG PRODUCTION, controlled by MANY genes and showing a continuous range of phenotypes, are described as ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Quantitative (polygenic)

mcq.q: The LAW OF SEGREGATION states that ... each parent
opts: One gene is always dominant in a heterozygous combination
Alleles separate during meiosis so offspring receive one allele from
Genes for different traits assort independently
A recessive allele is only expressed when masked
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Alleles separate during meiosis so offspring receive one allele from

mcq.q: Which of the following terms refers to a random, heritable change in an organism's DNA that introduces new genetic variation?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Mutation

mcq.q: The Estimated Breeding Value (EBV) formula is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. EBV = (Individual weight − Average weight) × Heritability %

mcq.q: Breeding of CLOSELY RELATED individuals (e.g. brother × sister) is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Inbreeding

mcq.q: Hybrid vigour (heterosis) results from which breeding system?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Crossbreeding

mcq.q: The process of inserting genes from one organism into the DNA of another to change its characteristics is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Genetic modification/engineering

mcq.q: Milk production in dairy cows is controlled by two gene pairs. Genotype aabbcc gives 200 litres; each additive dominant gene adds 20 litres. The milk yield for genotype AAbbcc is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. 240 litres

mcq.q: If a particular trait in livestock has a heritability estimate of 70%, what does this mean?
opts: 70% is determined genetically --- a high chance of being passed on
30% chance it will be passed on
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. 70% is determined genetically --- a high chance of being passed on

mcq.q: Identifying an animal for breeding based on the performance of its PARENTS AND GRANDPARENTS is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Pedigree selection

mcq.q: of his OFFSPRING is then evaluated. This is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Progeny testing

mcq.q: The reappearance of a characteristic that skipped a generation is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Atavism (throwback)

mcq.q: rather than the cow is said to show ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Prepotency

mcq.q: DELETION, INSERTION, SUBSTITUTION and INVERSION are all types of ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Gene mutation

mcq.q: When farmers continually mate GRADE animals to a PUREBRED sire over several generations to improve genetic quality, this is called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Upgrading

mcq.q: Mass selection in animal breeding involves ...
opts: Selecting breeding animals based solely on their pedigree records
Selecting breeding animals based on their own observable individual performance/phenotype (e.g. growth rate, milk yield)
Random mating with no selection criteria
Selecting only based on the performance of offspring
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mass selection is simple and relatively fast but does not account for genotype hidden by environmental effects.

mcq.q: Progeny testing in animal breeding involves ...
opts: Selecting an animal based only on its own performance
Evaluating a potential breeding animal (especially males) based on the average performance of its offspring
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Progeny testing gives a more reliable indication of an animal's true breeding value, especially for traits like milk yield that males cannot express themselves.

mcq.q: A breeding value (estimated breeding value, EBV) refers to ...
opts: The market price of an animal
An estimate of an animal's genetic merit for a particular trait, used to predict the performance of its future offspring
A measure of an animal's current body weight only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. EBVs help farmers select breeding stock likely to pass on desirable genetic traits to their offspring.

mcq.q: Genetic improvement of a livestock herd over generations is achieved mainly through ...
opts: Consistent selective breeding, choosing animals with the most desirable genotypes/phenotypes as parents of the next generation
Avoiding the use of any breeding records
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Genetic improvement programmes rely on accurately identifying and breeding from animals with superior genetic merit.

mcq.q: Culling, in the context of a livestock breeding programme, refers to ...
opts: Selecting the best animals to keep for breeding
Removing animals with undesirable traits (e.g. poor production, genetic defects, poor fertility) from the breeding herd
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Culling poor-performing or defective animals helps improve the overall genetic quality of the remaining herd.

mcq.q: In Hereford cattle, the polled (hornless) condition (P) is dominant over the horned condition (p). A heterozygous polled bull (Pp) is mated with a horned cow (pp). What proportion of the offspring is expected to be horned?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Pp × pp → 1 Pp (polled) : 1 pp (horned), giving a 50% chance of horned offspring.

mcq.q: In a particular sheep breed, white wool (W) is dominant over black wool (w). Two heterozygous (Ww) sheep are mated. What proportion of the offspring is expected to have black wool?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ww × Ww → 1 WW : 2 Ww : 1 ww, giving a 25% (1 in 4) chance of black wool (ww) offspring.

mcq.q: If a homozygous dominant polled bull (PP) is mated with a homozygous recessive horned cow (pp), what proportion of the F1 offspring will be horned?
opts: 0% — all offspring will be polled (heterozygous, Pp)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. PP × pp can only produce Pp offspring, which are all polled (since polled is dominant), despite all carrying the recessive allele.

mcq.q: A Punnett square is a tool used in genetics to ...
opts: Calculate the market value of livestock
Predict the probable genotype and phenotype ratios of offspring from a particular genetic cross
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Punnett squares systematically combine possible gametes from each parent to show all possible offspring genotype combinations.

mcq.q: In livestock breeding, a 'carrier' animal for a recessive genetic defect refers to an animal that is ...
opts: Homozygous dominant, showing no defect and unable to pass the allele on
Heterozygous, not showing the defect itself but capable of passing the recessive allele to its offspring
Homozygous recessive, showing the full defect
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Carriers are a hidden risk in breeding programmes since they can produce affected offspring if mated with another carrier.

mcq.q: Why is it important for a livestock breeder to identify and manage carrier animals for known recessive genetic defects?
opts: Carriers always show the defect themselves, making them easy to identify visually
Carriers appear normal but can pass the harmful recessive allele to offspring; if two carriers are mated, some offspring may be affected by the defect
Carrier status has no impact on breeding decisions
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Without genetic testing or pedigree analysis, carriers cannot be identified by appearance alone, making them a hidden breeding risk.

practice.q: Define each of the following terms: **(a)** Genotype
practice.memo:
• Genotype: the GENETIC make-up of an organism (allele combination), e.g. Bb
• Phenotype: the PHYSICAL/observable appearance of an organism resulting from its genotype, e.g. black coat colour
• Homozygous: an organism with TWO IDENTICAL alleles for a trait, e.g. BB or bb
• Heterozygous: an organism with TWO DIFFERENT alleles for a trait, e.g. Bb
• Dominant allele: an allele that MASKS the effect of another allele when present (represented with a capital letter)
• Recessive allele: an allele whose effect is MASKED by a dominant allele; only expressed when in homozygous form (lowercase letter)

practice.q: State Mendel's LAW OF DOMINANCE and LAW OF SEGREGATION.
practice.memo:
• Law of Dominance: when two different alleles for a trait are present, the DOMINANT allele will mask/hide the effect of the recessive allele in the phenotype
• Law of Segregation: during the formation of gametes (meiosis), the two alleles for a trait SEPARATE so that each gamete receives only ONE allele; offspring inherit one allele from EACH parent

practice.q: Define MONOHYBRID CROSSING.
practice.memo: • A genetic cross that studies the inheritance of ONE characteristic/trait controlled by ONE gene pair

practice.q: A homozygous BLACK bull (BB) is crossed with a homozygous WHITE cow (bb). Use a Punnett square to determine the GENOTYPE and PHENOTYPE of the F1 offspring.
practice.memo:
• Genotype of F1: ALL Bb (heterozygous) --- 2 marks
• Phenotype of F1: ALL BLACK (since B is dominant) --- 2 marks
• Correct Punnett square layout with gametes B and b correctly crossed --- 2 marks

practice.q: The F1 offspring from 4.2.2 are crossed with each other (Bb × Bb). Use a Punnett square to determine the genotypic AND phenotypic ratios of the F2 offspring.
practice.memo:
• Genotypic ratio: 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb
• Phenotypic ratio: 3 BLACK : 1 WHITE
• Correctly completed Punnett square showing all 4 combinations

practice.q: Distinguish between COMPLETE DOMINANCE, INCOMPLETE DOMINANCE and CO-DOMINANCE. Give ONE livestock example of each.
practice.memo:
• Complete dominance: ONE allele completely MASKS the other; heterozygote shows the dominant phenotype ONLY. Example: black (B) completely dominant over white (b) in cattle coat colour
• Incomplete dominance: NEITHER allele is completely dominant; heterozygote shows a BLENDED/INTERMEDIATE phenotype. Example: red × white flower → pink offspring
• Co-dominance: BOTH alleles are EQUALLY expressed, appearing TOGETHER (not blended) in the phenotype. Example: red bull × white cow → roan offspring (red and white hairs both visible)

practice.q: A homozygous RED cow (RR) is crossed with a homozygous WHITE bull (WW), producing ROAN offspring. **(a)** Name this pattern of inheritance.
practice.memo:
• (a) Co-dominance
• (b) Genotype of F1: RW (one allele for red, one for white --- both expressed)  ----------------------- ----------------------- -----------------------
• (c) Punnett square correctly completed (2 marks) --- Phenotypic ratio: 1 RED : 2 ROAN : 1 WHITE (4 marks)

practice.q: Define HERITABILITY and explain what a heritability value ABOVE 50% means for selection.
practice.memo:
• Heritability: the PROPORTION (percentage) of the variation in a characteristic that is due to GENETIC factors and can be passed on to offspring
• Above 50%: HIGH heritability --- the characteristic is strongly influenced by genetics, so SELECTION will be EFFECTIVE and rapid genetic progress can be made

practice.q: A beef farmer has the following information about a bull: -------------------------------------- -------------------------------- **DETAIL** **VALUE** Weaning weight of selected bull 320 kg Average weaning weight of all bulls in 280 kg the herd Heritability of weaning weight 45% -------------------------------------- -------------------------------- **(a)** Use the EBV formula to calculate the Estimated Breeding Value.
practice.memo:
• EBV = (Individual weight − Average weight) × Heritability %
• EBV = (320 − 280) × 0,45 = 40 × 0,45 = 18 kg
• Expected weaning weight of offspring = Herd average + EBV = 280 + 18 = 298 kg
• Interpretation: since the EBV is POSITIVE (+18 kg), the offspring's weaning weight is expected to be ABOVE the herd average --- this bull should be selected for breeding

practice.q: Describe the following methods of selection and give ONE advantage of each: **(a)** Pedigree selection
practice.memo:
• Pedigree selection: animals are selected based on the performance/records of their ANCESTORS (parents and grandparents). Advantage: can be done EARLY in the animal's life before its own performance is known
• Progeny testing: a sire is mated to several females and its breeding value is determined based on the performance of its OFFSPRING. Advantage: gives the MOST ACCURATE/reliable estimate of breeding value

practice.q: Distinguish between INBREEDING and CROSSBREEDING. Give ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage of each.
practice.memo:
• Inbreeding: mating of CLOSELY RELATED animals (e.g. sire × daughter). Advantage: FIXES desirable characteristics in the herd. Disadvantage: causes INBREEDING DEPRESSION --- reduced fertility, vigour and increased genetic defects
• Crossbreeding: mating animals of DIFFERENT breeds. Advantage: produces HYBRID VIGOUR (heterosis) --- improved growth, fertility and disease resistance. Disadvantage: offspring CANNOT be used for further pure breeding (loses uniformity)

practice.q: Define HYBRID VIGOUR (heterosis) and explain its benefit in commercial beef production.
practice.memo:
• Hybrid vigour (heterosis): the phenomenon where CROSSBRED offspring perform BETTER than either of their purebred parents in traits such as growth rate, fertility and disease resistance
• Benefit: commercial beef producers use crossbreeding to produce calves with FASTER growth rates and HIGHER weaning weights, increasing profitability

practice.q: Distinguish between mass selection and progeny testing as methods of selecting breeding animals.
practice.memo:
• Mass selection: animals are selected based on their OWN individual performance/phenotype ✓✓
• Progeny testing: animals (especially males) are selected based on the average performance of their OFFSPRING, giving a more accurate picture of their true genetic/breeding value ✓✓

practice.q: In Hereford cattle, polled (hornless, P) is dominant over horned (p). A heterozygous polled bull (Pp) is mated with a heterozygous polled cow (Pp). Using a Punnett square, determine the expected genotype and phenotype ratio of the offspring.
practice.memo:
• Cross: Pp × Pp ✓
• Gametes: P, p from each parent ✓
• Punnett square: PP, Pp, Pp, pp ✓✓
• Genotype ratio: 1 PP : 2 Pp : 1 pp ✓
• Phenotype ratio: 3 polled : 1 horned ✓

practice.q: Explain why two outwardly normal, healthy parent animals can sometimes produce offspring with a genetic defect, and explain how a breeder can reduce this risk.
practice.memo:
• Both parents may be heterozygous CARRIERS of a recessive allele causing the defect, without showing the defect themselves ✓✓
• When two carriers are mated, there is a chance (e.g. 25% for a simple recessive trait) that an offspring will inherit two copies of the recessive allele and be affected ✓
• Risk can be reduced through genetic testing/screening of breeding animals and careful pedigree record-keeping to avoid mating two known/suspected carriers ✓

practice.q: Discuss how consistent selective breeding over several generations can improve the overall production performance of a dairy herd.
practice.memo:
• Only animals with the most desirable traits (e.g. high milk yield, good fertility, disease resistance) are selected as parents of the next generation ✓✓
• Over successive generations, the frequency of alleles for desirable traits increases in the herd ✓
• This leads to a gradual, cumulative improvement in the average genetic merit/production performance of the herd over time ✓
